chunks.title: Properties of α-cut
chunks.content: The slide presents properties and formulas related to α-cuts in fuzzy sets, including operations on unions and intersections of fuzzy sets, subset and equality conditions, and relationships between different levels of α-cuts.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide presents properties and formulas related to α-cuts in fuzzy sets, including operations on unions and intersections of fuzzy sets, subset and equality conditions, and relationships between different levels of α-cuts.

chunks.content: For a fuzzy set A, the α-cut is defined as the crisp set containing all elements of the universal set whose membership values in A are greater than or equal to α.
chunks.embedding_text: α-cut: For a fuzzy set A, the α-cut is defined as the crisp set containing all elements of the universal set whose membership values in A are greater than or equal to α.

chunks.content: For a fuzzy set A, the α+ cut is defined as the crisp set containing all elements of the universal set whose membership values in A are strictly greater than α.
chunks.embedding_text: α+ cut: For a fuzzy set A, the α+ cut is defined as the crisp set containing all elements of the universal set whose membership values in A are strictly greater than α.
∪(αAi) ⊆ (∪Ai)α and ∩(αAi) = (∩Ai)α
∪(α+Ai) = (∪Ai)α+ and ∩(α+Ai) ⊆ (∩Ai)α+
A ⊆ B iff αA ⊆ αB
A ⊆ B iff α+A ⊆ α+B
A = B iff αA = αB
A = B iff α+A = α+B
αA = ∩(β<α) βA = ∩(β<α) β+A
α+A = ∪(α<β) βA = ∪(α<β) β+A

chunks.title: Representation of fuzzy sets
chunks.content: The slide explains the representation of fuzzy sets using α-level sets (also known as level cuts or cross-sections). It introduces the concept of a special fuzzy set and its decomposition into α-level sets over the interval [0,1] or the set of all α-levels Λ(A).
chunks.raw_text:
Representation of fuzzy sets

► Special fuzzy set:
αA(x) = α · ^αA(x).

► So:
A = ∪ α∈[0,1] αA,
A = ∪ α∈Λ(A) αA
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the representation of fuzzy sets using α-level sets (also known as level cuts or cross-sections). It introduces the concept of a special fuzzy set and its decomposition into α-level sets over the interval [0,1] or the set of all α-levels Λ(A).

chunks.content: A crisp set containing all elements of the universal set whose membership degree in a fuzzy set A is greater than or equal to α.
chunks.embedding_text: α-level set (α-cut): A crisp set containing all elements of the universal set whose membership degree in a fuzzy set A is greater than or equal to α.

chunks.content: The set of all α-levels for a fuzzy set A.
chunks.embedding_text: Λ(A): The set of all α-levels for a fuzzy set A.
αA(x) = α · ^αA(x)
A = ∪ α∈Λ(A) αA

chunks.title: Extension principle of Fuzzy sets
chunks.content: The slide explains the extension principle of fuzzy sets, detailing how a crisp function can be extended to fuzzy sets and the mathematical definitions for the extended function and its inverse.
chunks.raw_text:
Extension principle of Fuzzy sets

▶ If a crisp function

f : X → Y

▶ We can define:

f : F(X) → F(Y),

f⁻¹ : F(Y) → F(X).

▶ Where

[f(A)](y) = sup A(x)
          x: y = f(x)

[f⁻¹(B)](x) = B(f(x))
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the extension principle of fuzzy sets, detailing how a crisp function can be extended to fuzzy sets and the mathematical definitions for the extended function and its inverse.

chunks.content: A principle that allows the extension of a crisp function to fuzzy sets by defining new fuzzy functions and their inverses.
chunks.embedding_text: Extension principle of fuzzy sets: A principle that allows the extension of a crisp function to fuzzy sets by defining new fuzzy functions and their inverses.

chunks.content: A function that maps elements from one set to another without any degree of membership ambiguity.
chunks.embedding_text: Crisp function: A function that maps elements from one set to another without any degree of membership ambiguity.

chunks.title: Operations of fuzzy sets
chunks.content: The slide introduces the standard operations of fuzzy sets, including complement, intersection, and union, and explains that these operations can be redefined as t-norms and t-conorms based on specific problem requirements.
chunks.raw_text:
Operations of fuzzy sets

▶ Recall that standard operations are

A̅(x) = 1 - A(x),
(A ∩ B)(x) = min[A(x), B(x)],
(A ∪ B)(x) = max[A(x), B(x)]

▶ We can redefine the operations based on the problem we have
▶ We usually call fuzzy intersection: t-norm
▶ We usually call fuzzy union t-conorm
chunks.embedding_text: The slide introduces the standard operations of fuzzy sets, including complement, intersection, and union, and explains that these operations can be redefined as t-norms and t-conorms based on specific problem requirements.

chunks.embedding_text: Complement of a fuzzy set: The complement of a fuzzy set A(x) is defined as 1 - A(x).

chunks.content: A generalized intersection operation for fuzzy sets, often replacing the standard min function.
chunks.embedding_text: t-norm: A generalized intersection operation for fuzzy sets, often replacing the standard min function.

chunks.content: A generalized union operation for fuzzy sets, often replacing the standard max function.
chunks.embedding_text: t-conorm: A generalized union operation for fuzzy sets, often replacing the standard max function.

chunks.title: Fuzzy complements
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of fuzzy complements, detailing the properties and axioms that a complement function must satisfy in fuzzy logic. It emphasizes the mathematical formulation and conditions for a function to be considered a valid complement function in the context of membership degrees.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy complements

► Any complement is a function of membership:

c : [0, 1] → [0, 1],

► Should satisfy:

Axiom c1. c(0) = 1 and c(1) = 0 (boundary conditions).
Axiom c2. For all a, b ∈ [0, 1], if a ≤ b, then c(a) ≥ c(b) (monotonicity).
Axiom c3. c is a continuous function.
Axiom c4. c is involutive, which means that c(c(a)) = a for each a ∈ [0, 1].
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of fuzzy complements, detailing the properties and axioms that a complement function must satisfy in fuzzy logic. It emphasizes the mathematical formulation and conditions for a function to be considered a valid complement function in the context of membership degrees.

chunks.content: A function of membership that maps values from the interval [0, 1] to [0, 1], satisfying specific axioms.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy complement: A function of membership that maps values from the interval [0, 1] to [0, 1], satisfying specific axioms.

chunks.content: Conditions where the complement function maps 0 to 1 and 1 to 0.
chunks.embedding_text: Boundary conditions: Conditions where the complement function maps 0 to 1 and 1 to 0.

chunks.content: A property where if a ≤ b, then c(a) ≥ c(b) for all a, b in [0, 1].
chunks.embedding_text: Monotonicity: A property where if a ≤ b, then c(a) ≥ c(b) for all a, b in [0, 1].

chunks.content: A function where applying it twice returns the original input, i.e., c(c(a)) = a for each a in [0, 1].
chunks.embedding_text: Involutive function: A function where applying it twice returns the original input, i.e., c(c(a)) = a for each a in [0, 1].

chunks.formula: if a ≤ b, then c(a) ≥ c(b)
chunks.embedding_text: if a ≤ b, then c(a) ≥ c(b)
c(c(a)) = a

chunks.title: Complement Examples
chunks.content: The slide provides examples of different complement functions used in mathematical logic or fuzzy logic, including threshold, cosine, Sugemo, and Yager functions. Each function is defined with its respective formula.
chunks.raw_text:
Complement Examples

▶ Threshold
c(a) = { 1 for a ≤ t, 0 for a > t }

▶ Cosine (not involutive)
c(a) = 1/2 (1 + cos πa)

▶ Sugemo
c_λ(a) = (1 - a) / (1 + λa)

▶ Yager
c_w(a) = (1 - a^w)^(1/w)
chunks.embedding_text: The slide provides examples of different complement functions used in mathematical logic or fuzzy logic, including threshold, cosine, Sugemo, and Yager functions. Each function is defined with its respective formula.

chunks.embedding_text: Sugemo Function: A complement function parameterized by λ, defined as (1 - a) / (1 + λa).

chunks.title: How to generate Complements
chunks.content: The slide explains the methods to generate complements in fuzzy logic using specific functions with given conditions. It provides two formulas to calculate complements based on whether a function is increasing or strictly decreasing.
chunks.raw_text:
How to generate Complements

▶ If g(0)=0 and g is increasing then:

c(a) = g⁻¹(g(1) - g(a))

▶ Is a fuzzy complement

▶ If f(1) = 0 and is strictly decreasing:

c(a) = f⁻¹(f(0) - f(a))

chunks.title: Fuzzy intersection: t-norm
chunks.content: The slide introduces the concept of fuzzy intersection using a t-norm function. It explains that a t-norm is a function that maps pairs of values in the interval [0, 1] to another value in the same interval, used to determine the intersection of two fuzzy sets.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy intersection: t-norm

▶ Is a function:

  i : [0, 1] × [0, 1] → [0, 1].

  (A ∩ B)(x) = i[A(x), B(x)]
chunks.embedding_text: The slide introduces the concept of fuzzy intersection using a t-norm function. It explains that a t-norm is a function that maps pairs of values in the interval [0, 1] to another value in the same interval, used to determine the intersection of two fuzzy sets.

chunks.embedding_text: t-norm: A function i: [0, 1] × [0, 1] → [0, 1] used to compute the intersection of two fuzzy sets.

chunks.title: T-norm restrictions
chunks.content: The slide outlines seven axioms that define the properties and restrictions of T-norms, which are functions used in fuzzy logic and multi-criteria decision-making. These axioms include boundary conditions, monotonicity, commutativity, associativity, continuity, subidempotency, and strict monotonicity.
chunks.raw_text:
T-norm restrictions

Axiom i1. i(a, 1) = a (boundary condition).
Axiom i2. b ≤ d implies i(a, b) ≤ i(a, d) (monotonicity).
Axiom i3. i(a, b) = i(b, a) (commutativity).
Axiom i4. i(a, i(b, d)) = i(i(a, b), d) (associativity).
Axiom i5. i is a continuous function (continuity).
Axiom i6. i(a, a) < a (subidempotency).
Axiom i7. a1 < a2 and b1 < b2 implies i(a1, b1) < i(a2, b2) (strict monotonicity).
chunks.embedding_text: The slide outlines seven axioms that define the properties and restrictions of T-norms, which are functions used in fuzzy logic and multi-criteria decision-making. These axioms include boundary conditions, monotonicity, commutativity, associativity, continuity, subidempotency, and strict monotonicity.

chunks.content: The condition that i(a, 1) equals a.
chunks.embedding_text: Boundary Condition: The condition that i(a, 1) equals a.

chunks.content: If b ≤ d, then i(a, b) ≤ i(a, d).
chunks.embedding_text: Monotonicity: If b ≤ d, then i(a, b) ≤ i(a, d).

chunks.content: The function i(a, b) is equal to i(b, a).
chunks.embedding_text: Commutativity: The function i(a, b) is equal to i(b, a).

chunks.content: The function satisfies i(a, i(b, d)) = i(i(a, b), d).
chunks.embedding_text: Associativity: The function satisfies i(a, i(b, d)) = i(i(a, b), d).

chunks.content: The function i is continuous.
chunks.embedding_text: Continuity: The function i is continuous.

chunks.content: The function satisfies i(a, a) < a.
chunks.embedding_text: Subidempotency: The function satisfies i(a, a) < a.

chunks.content: If a1 < a2 and b1 < b2, then i(a1, b1) < i(a2, b2).
chunks.embedding_text: Strict Monotonicity: If a1 < a2 and b1 < b2, then i(a1, b1) < i(a2, b2).

i(a, b) = i(b, a)

i is continuous

chunks.title: T-norm examples
chunks.content: The slide provides various examples of T-norms, which are functions used in fuzzy logic to model logical conjunction. It includes definitions and formulas for standard intersection, algebraic product, bounded difference, drastic intersection, and Yager class t-norms.
chunks.raw_text:
T-norm examples

Standard intersection : i(a, b) = min(a, b).
Algebraic product : i(a, b) = ab.
Bounded difference : i(a, b) = max(0, a + b - 1).
Drastic intersection : i(a, b) = { a when b = 1, b when a = 1, 0 otherwise. 
Yager class t-norms:
i_w(a, b) = 1 - min(1, [(1 - a)^w + (1 - b)^w]^1/w) (w > 0).
chunks.embedding_text: The slide provides various examples of T-norms, which are functions used in fuzzy logic to model logical conjunction. It includes definitions and formulas for standard intersection, algebraic product, bounded difference, drastic intersection, and Yager class t-norms.

chunks.title: Fuzzy union: t-conorm
chunks.content: The slide introduces the concept of a fuzzy union using a t-conorm (t-conormative) function, which is a specific type of function used in fuzzy set theory to combine membership values of two fuzzy sets.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy union: t-conorm

▶ It is a function

u : [0, 1] × [0, 1] → [0, 1].

(A ∪ B)(x) = u[A(x), B(x)]
chunks.embedding_text: The slide introduces the concept of a fuzzy union using a t-conorm (t-conormative) function, which is a specific type of function used in fuzzy set theory to combine membership values of two fuzzy sets.

chunks.content: A function u that takes two values from the interval [0, 1] and returns a value in [0, 1], used to compute the union of two fuzzy sets.
chunks.embedding_text: t-conorm: A function u that takes two values from the interval [0, 1] and returns a value in [0, 1], used to compute the union of two fuzzy sets.

chunks.title: Fuzzy operation duality
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of duality in fuzzy operations, particularly focusing on De Morgan laws in crisp sets and their extension to fuzzy operations. It highlights that not all fuzzy operations satisfy De Morgan laws and introduces the duality between t-norms and t-conorms with respect to a complement operation.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy operation duality

► In crisp sets: De Morgan laws

A̅ ∩ B̅ = A̅ ∪ B̅ and A̅ ∪ B̅ = A̅ ∩ B̅.

► It is clear that some fuzzy operations but not all may satisfy the above low
► t-norm i and t-conorm u are dual with respect to complement c iff:

c(i(a, b)) = u(c(a), c(b))

c(u(a, b)) = i(c(a), c(b)).
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of duality in fuzzy operations, particularly focusing on De Morgan laws in crisp sets and their extension to fuzzy operations. It highlights that not all fuzzy operations satisfy De Morgan laws and introduces the duality between t-norms and t-conorms with respect to a complement operation.

chunks.content: Laws stating that the complement of the intersection of two sets is equal to the union of their complements, and vice versa.
chunks.embedding_text: De Morgan laws (in crisp sets): Laws stating that the complement of the intersection of two sets is equal to the union of their complements, and vice versa.

chunks.content: A function that generalizes logical conjunction to fuzzy sets.
chunks.embedding_text: t-norm: A function that generalizes logical conjunction to fuzzy sets.

chunks.content: A function that generalizes logical disjunction to fuzzy sets.
chunks.embedding_text: t-conorm: A function that generalizes logical disjunction to fuzzy sets.

chunks.content: An operation that maps a membership value to its complement in the context of fuzzy sets.
chunks.embedding_text: Complement (c): An operation that maps a membership value to its complement in the context of fuzzy sets.
